title: Three Conflicts Inside One War
subtitle: The Fragmentation of the U.S.–Israel–Iran Conflict into U.S.–Iran, Iran–Israel, and U.S.–Israel Tracks

creators.name: Wu, Shaoyuan
creators.affiliation: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC

alternate_identifiers.description: Short form of the policy brief title
Core analytical model developed in the policy brief
Central outcome pattern identified in the policy brief

description: This policy brief argues that the U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict is no longer best understood as a single integrated war moving toward a single settlement. It analyzes the conflict as three connected but differentiated tracks: U.S.–Iran termination, Iran–Israel deterrence, and U.S.–Israel alliance management. The brief concludes that the most plausible outcome is controlled fragmentation, in which a U.S.–Iran memorandum may reduce direct escalation while unresolved deterrence, proxy, maritime, nuclear, and alliance-management pressures continue through separate but linked channels.
abstract: This policy brief argues that the U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict is no longer best understood as a single integrated war moving toward a single settlement. Instead, it is fragmenting into three connected but differentiated tracks: U.S.–Iran termination, Iran–Israel deterrence, and U.S.–Israel alliance management. The U.S.–Iran track is moving toward bargaining, sequencing, and procedural de-escalation; the Iran–Israel track remains focused on deterrence, retaliation, and proxy control; and the U.S.–Israel track increasingly involves alliance management, conditionality, and political synchronization. The brief argues that a U.S.–Iran memorandum may reduce direct military escalation without resolving the broader conflict system. Unresolved leverage may migrate into nuclear talks, maritime rules, proxy fronts, U.S.–Israel consultations, and competing domestic narratives. The most plausible outcome is therefore controlled fragmentation: direct U.S.–Iran de-escalation, bounded Iran–Israel competition, conditional U.S.–Israel synchronization, and continued monitoring of recovery competition after any ceasefire.

series_information.series_name: EPINOVA Policy Brief Series on Strategic Competition, AI-Enabled Warfare, and Information Conflict
series_information.series_description: Policy brief series translating EPINOVA research on strategic competition, AI-enabled warfare, information conflict, systemic risk, logistics resilience, and global security governance into concise policy-facing analysis.

methodology.source_basis: Open-source policy analysis, prior EPINOVA analytical frameworks, and scenario-based interpretation of reported diplomatic developments as of June 14, 2026. The brief treats reported memorandum terms as provisional unless formally released or acknowledged by the negotiating parties.
methodology.case_context: The U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict under reported U.S.–Iran memorandum negotiations and emerging fragmentation across U.S.–Iran, Iran–Israel, and U.S.–Israel tracks.
methodology.core_problem: The central analytical problem is whether a reported U.S.–Iran memorandum can govern a conflict system that is fragmenting into differentiated termination, deterrence, and alliance-management tracks rather than converging toward one integrated settlement.
central_claims: The U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict is fragmenting into three connected but differentiated tracks: U.S.–Iran termination, Iran–Israel deterrence, and U.S.–Israel alliance management.
A U.S.–Iran memorandum would manage escalation rather than resolve the conflict system.
The United States and Iran require different forms of success, making dual narrative construction a condition for implementation.
Israel faces the most constrained strategic position because a U.S.–Iran memorandum may satisfy Washington’s requirements before satisfying Israel’s deterrence requirements.
The most plausible outcome is controlled fragmentation rather than comprehensive peace.
The central policy challenge is governing the tracks, not merely signing a memorandum.
The main relationship risk is conditionalization rather than alliance collapse.

analytical_lenses.name: Triangular Conflict Decomposition
analytical_lenses.description: Frames a conflict structure in which three strategically connected actors no longer operate under a single war aim, escalation logic, or termination pathway.

analytical_lenses.name: U.S.–Iran Termination Track
analytical_lenses.description: Analyzes bargaining, sequencing, exit architecture, nuclear restraint, sanctions relief, maritime stabilization, and pressure-backed diplomacy.

analytical_lenses.name: Iran–Israel Deterrence Track
analytical_lenses.description: Analyzes residual deterrence pressure involving nuclear, missile, proxy, cyber, and covert networks that cannot be fully resolved by a bilateral U.S.–Iran memorandum.

analytical_lenses.name: U.S.–Israel Alliance-Management Track
analytical_lenses.description: Assesses divergence within alignment, including the distinction between Israeli security and Israeli escalation preferences.

analytical_lenses.name: Controlled Fragmentation
analytical_lenses.description: Identifies a realistic outcome in which direct U.S.–Iran escalation declines while bounded Iran–Israel competition and conditional U.S.–Israel synchronization continue.

analytical_lenses.name: Narrative Compatibility Analysis
analytical_lenses.description: Examines whether Washington, Tehran, and Israel can frame the same agreement in different but politically usable terms without undermining implementation.

analytical_lenses.name: Implementation Risk Migration
analytical_lenses.description: Tracks how unresolved leverage may migrate from direct hostilities into technical talks, maritime rules, proxy fronts, alliance consultations, and competing domestic narratives.

dimensions.name: U.S.–Iran Termination Track
dimensions.observed_pattern: The U.S.–Iran track is moving toward bargaining, sequencing, nuclear restraint, sanctions relief, maritime stabilization, and procedural de-escalation.
dimensions.interpretation: This track can reduce direct escalation but is unlikely to resolve the broader conflict system.

dimensions.name: Iran–Israel Deterrence Track
dimensions.observed_pattern: Israel remains focused on deterrence restoration, while Iran seeks to avoid appearing to abandon retaliation, Hezbollah, or the broader resistance network.
dimensions.interpretation: This track remains the least compatible with clean de-escalation and may continue through Lebanon, Syria, cyber, covert, and proxy channels.

dimensions.name: U.S.–Israel Alliance-Management Track
dimensions.observed_pattern: The United States remains committed to Israeli security but increasingly needs controlled recompression, while Israel prioritizes deterrence restoration.
dimensions.interpretation: Alignment remains durable but less synchronized, creating risk of conditionalization rather than alliance rupture.

dimensions.name: Procedural Stabilization
dimensions.observed_pattern: A reported memorandum would reduce immediate escalation while deferring the most difficult issues.
dimensions.interpretation: The memorandum functions as a conflict-management device, not a comprehensive settlement.

dimensions.name: Narrative Incompatibility
dimensions.observed_pattern: Washington may frame the agreement as pressure-backed diplomacy, Tehran as resistance and recovery, and Israel as insufficient constraint.
dimensions.interpretation: Competing narratives may coexist initially but could generate later compliance disputes.

dimensions.name: Proxy Autonomy
dimensions.observed_pattern: Hezbollah, Iraqi militias, the Houthis, or other non-state partners may retain local incentives that do not fully align with Tehran’s preference for restraint.
dimensions.interpretation: Proxy behavior may remain a major source of implementation risk even if Tehran accepts de-escalation.

dimensions.name: Resource-Chain Continuation
dimensions.observed_pattern: Economic pressure may persist in fertilizer inputs, LNG, petrochemicals, metals, battery materials, insurance costs, and shipping confidence even if oil flows stabilize.
dimensions.interpretation: The economic consequences of the conflict may outlast the visible kinetic phase.

key_findings: The U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict is increasingly difficult to interpret as a single integrated war system.
The conflict is separating into three connected but differentiated tracks: U.S.–Iran termination, Iran–Israel deterrence, and U.S.–Israel alliance management.
A U.S.–Iran memorandum could reduce direct military escalation but would more likely move unresolved disputes into a managed framework than resolve the conflict system.
Washington requires a credible exit narrative centered on Hormuz, nuclear risk reduction, cost control, and alliance credibility.
Tehran requires visible material and political returns, including asset release, oil-export access, sanctions relief, sovereignty language, nuclear space, and preservation of regional leverage.
Israel faces the most constrained strategic position because Washington increasingly needs termination or de-escalation while Israel continues to prioritize deterrence restoration.
The most plausible outcome is controlled fragmentation rather than comprehensive peace.
The central policy challenge is governing the tracks, not merely signing a memorandum.

The main risk is conditionalization rather than alliance collapse: U.S. support for Israel and Iranian support for Hezbollah may remain formally intact while becoming more selective, constrained, or conditional in practice.
policy_recommendations: Treat war termination as system design rather than simply as the cessation of strikes.
Separate nuclear sequencing from nuclear surrender through staged freeze, declaration, monitoring, downblending or disposition, enrichment limits, inspection architecture, and compliance review.
Build regional side channels into the settlement architecture, including Lebanon-related escalation, Israeli operations, resupply visibility, and deconfliction mechanisms.
Distinguish Israeli security from Israeli escalation preferences when managing U.S.–Israel coordination during U.S.–Iran war termination.
Make benefits for Iran visible, conditional, and reversible through benchmarks, monitoring, and snapback provisions.

Prepare for controlled fragmentation rather than full reconciliation, including direct U.S.–Iran de-escalation, bounded Iran–Israel competition, conditional U.S.–Israel synchronization, and continued monitoring of recovery competition.

watchpoints.indicator: Formal U.S.–Iran de-escalation accompanied by informal Iran–Israel continuation through Lebanon, Syria, Iraq, Yemen, covert operations, or cyber channels
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Detects whether the memorandum reduces direct escalation without ending the broader conflict system.

watchpoints.indicator: Israeli late-stage escalation against proxy infrastructure, supply corridors, leadership targets, or nuclear-adjacent systems
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Assesses whether Israel seeks to alter the bargaining environment before implementation becomes locked in.

watchpoints.indicator: Iranian hardline resistance to implementation or demands for stronger guarantees and visible gains
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Tracks whether Tehran can balance compliance with domestic and institutional credibility.

watchpoints.indicator: Proxy operations by Hezbollah, Iraqi militias, the Houthis, or other actors despite formal de-escalation
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Evaluates whether proxy autonomy becomes a major source of implementation risk.

watchpoints.indicator: Competing U.S., Iranian, and Israeli narratives around the same memorandum terms
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Identifies whether narrative incompatibility generates later compliance disputes.

watchpoints.indicator: Continued volatility in fertilizer inputs, LNG, petrochemicals, metals, battery materials, insurance costs, and shipping confidence
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Monitors whether economic consequences outlast the visible kinetic phase.

watchpoints.indicator: Russia, China, and Iran deepening selective coordination through corridor redundancy, sanctions adaptation, maritime-order contestation, or selective economic absorption
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Assesses whether the conflict accelerates latent strategic alignment without producing a formal bloc.

tables.title: Implementation Risks Under a U.S.–Iran Memorandum
tables.description: Summarizes implementation risks under a U.S.–Iran memorandum, including formal U.S.–Iran de-escalation with informal Iran–Israel continuation, Israeli late-stage escalation, Iranian internal backlash, proxy autonomy, narrative incompatibility, resource-chain continuation, and Eurasian adaptation.
tables.source: Author’s analysis based on CFR, Reuters, The Guardian, and prior EPINOVA research.
tables.note: The table assesses implementation risk rather than probability of memorandum collapse. Triangular conflict decomposition refers to the separation of the U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict into three connected but differentiated tracks: U.S.–Iran termination, Iran–Israel deterrence, and U.S.–Israel alliance management.

funding.statement: No external funding information is provided in the PDF.

software.statement: No software artifact is specified in the PDF.

disclaimer: This policy brief is an institutional publication of EPINOVA, prepared by Dr. Shaoyuan Wu in his capacity as Director of the Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC. The analysis is based on publicly available information and scenario-based analytical estimates and does not represent the official position of any government. The publication is intended solely for research and policy discussion purposes and does not constitute legal, military, operational, or sanctions-compliance advice.
